symbols: massive apse and rounded forms — the Romanesque emphasis on solidity, permanence, and the sheltering embrace of the Church
dome crowned with a cross — the heavenly vault under which the faithful gather, pointing upward to God
baptistery tower visible at left — the sacrament of initiation into the Christian community, the gateway to spiritual life
arched gallery along the apse — rhythmic repetition of arches symbolizing the ordered beauty of creation and divine harmony
open piazza surrounding the cathedral — the public, communal nature of Christian worship, the Church at the center of civic life
description: An exterior photograph of the Parma Cathedral (Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta) in Parma, Italy, viewed primarily from the east showing the monumental rounded apse. The Romanesque structure is built of warm-toned brick and stone, with a series of blind arcades and arched galleries running along the exterior walls. A green copper dome rises above the crossing. To the far left, the distinctive octagonal baptistery is partially visible, clad in pink Verona marble and currently under scaffolding. People walk across the paved piazza in the foreground, giving scale to the enormous building.
